title: An effective worldvolume action for the information world-tube: dynamical completion, a closed-timelike-curve dissipation model, and routes to falsifiability
author: Yin, Xiang
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: A recent series of works re-described stored quantum information as an extended  information world-tube  whose spacelike cross section is an area-quantized region, recovered the familiar worldline as the derived eikonal centroid of that tube, placed the discrete-spacetime information network of loop quantum gravity (LQG) and a semiconductor quantum-dot array in a common renormalization-group universality class, and classified self-consistent closed-timelike-curve (CTC) histories into two families. Three fronts were explicitly left open: the  dynamics  of the tube, the  computation  of the LQG-side universality invariants, and an  empirical signature . Here we take steps on all three, analytically and programmatically. First, we propose an effective worldvolume action—a Nambu–Goto/Dirac membrane term supplemented by an area–information constraint—whose extremization makes the world-tube a worldvolume of stationary area, and whose thin-tube (eikonal) reduction returns, at leading order, the timelike centroid geodesic and the Ehrenfest relation previously posited only kinematically; the leading correction couples the centroid to the background curvature through the cross-sectional quadrupole, in the manner of an extended body. Second, we record a concrete protocol for extracting the topological entanglement entropy and the entanglement spectrum from spin-network reduced density matrices, turning an unfilled component of the universality vector into a well-defined numerical task. Third, we solve a minimal single-qubit model of the post-selected “rewind” route, obtaining a closed-form Landauer entropy production that is monotone in the rewind parameter and that reproduces the loss of retained future memory. We then identify a parametric, presently inaccessible high-energy-scattering signature of the finite transverse cross section, and we set out the remaining numerical and experimental steps as a research program. Every result below is an analytical proposal or a protocol; we make no claim of numerical or experimental confirmation, and we stress that the framework remains a theoretical proposal.
